title: Escovitch fish
subtitle: Jamaican fried fish with pickled pepper sauce
excerpt: Firm white fish fried until crisp and golden, then covered with a hot, tangy sauce of sliced peppers, onion, and vinegar and left to marinate for an hour. A Jamaican dish with deep Sephardic roots. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Escovitch fish derives from the Spanish escabeche technique (frying fish and preserving it in spiced vinegar), brought to Jamaica by Sephardic Jewish traders in the 16th century. Documented by Enid Donaldson in The Real Taste of Jamaica (1993). The Jamaican version adds scotch bonnet, allspice, and a rainbow of peppers to the pickling mixture.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The dish improves with time — make it up to four hours ahead and leave at room temperature for the flavours to meld.

content.text: Escovitch fish is a hot-marinade dish: the sauce goes on while the fish is still warm, and the vinegar and spices penetrate the flesh as it cools. The fish is traditionally served at room temperature after an hour's resting, which is when the pickling has done its work. Eaten straight from the pan, it is good; eaten after an hour, it is much better.

items.prepNote: fillets or whole small fish, scaled and cleaned

content.text: Season the fish with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Dust lightly with {{plain-flour}} on both sides. Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of sunflower oil in a wide frying pan over medium-high heat. Fry the fish for 3–4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through. Remove to a serving dish and keep warm.

Discard most of the frying oil, leaving about 2 tablespoons in the pan. Add {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and fry the sliced onion, peppers, and scotch bonnet over medium heat for 3–4 minutes until slightly softened but still with bite. Add {{allspice}} of allspice and pour in {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar. Let it bubble for 30 seconds, then pour the hot sauce over the fried fish. Leave to cool and absorb for at least 1 hour before serving. Serve at room temperature with rice.

items.cause: Not cooked enough on the first side, or pan not hot enough to form a crust.
items.fix: Let the fish cook undisturbed for 3–4 minutes on the first side. It will release naturally from the pan when a crust has formed. If it sticks, it is not ready to turn.

items.symptom: Sauce too sharp and vinegary after marinating
items.cause: Too much vinegar, or the fish sat in the sauce for too long.
items.fix: The 80 ml of vinegar is correct for an hour's marinating. If making further ahead, taste after an hour — the acidity mutes with time.

content.text: Escovitch fish carries its history in the name: the word comes from the Spanish escabeche, a technique of frying and then preserving fish in spiced vinegar that was brought to Jamaica by Sephardic Jewish merchants in the 16th century. In a hot climate without refrigeration, the vinegar preservation extended the life of the fish. In modern Jamaica the technique is used for its flavour rather than its preservation properties, and the dish is eaten fresh the same day. It appears at breakfast, lunch, and dinner, and at the roadside stalls that line the Jamaican coastal roads near the fish markets.
